[Eggnog](/wiki/Eggnog "Eggnog") is a [sweetened](/wiki/Added_sugar "Added sugar") [dairy](/wiki/Dairy "Dairy")-based [beverage](/wiki/Sweetened_beverage "Sweetened beverage") traditionally made with milk, cream, sugar, and whipped eggs (which gives it a frothy texture). [Spirits](/wiki/Distilled_beverage "Distilled beverage") such as brandy, rum, or bourbon are often added. The finished serving is often garnished with a sprinkling of ground cinnamon or nutmeg.

### Cards

Main article: [Christmas card](/wiki/Christmas_card "Christmas card")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Christmas_postcard_1907.jpg/250px-Christmas_postcard_1907.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christmas_postcard_1907.jpg)

A 1907 Christmas card with [Santa](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus") and some of his reindeer

Christmas cards are illustrated messages of greeting exchanged between friends and family members during the weeks preceding Christmas Day. The traditional greeting reads "wishing you a Merry Christmas and a Happy New Year", much like that of the first commercial [Christmas card](/wiki/Christmas_card#History "Christmas card"), produced by [Sir Henry Cole](/wiki/Henry_Cole_(inventor) "Henry Cole (inventor)") in London in 1843.[[180]](#cite_note-181) The custom of sending them has become popular among a wide cross-section of people with the emergence of the modern trend towards exchanging [E-cards](/wiki/E-card "E-card").[[181]](#cite_note-182)[[182]](#cite_note-183)

Christmas cards are purchased in considerable quantities and feature artwork, commercially designed and relevant to the season. The content of the design might relate directly to the [Christmas narrative](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus"), with [depictions of the Nativity of Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus_in_art "Nativity of Jesus in art"), or [Christian symbols](/wiki/Christian_symbols "Christian symbols") such as the [Star of Bethlehem](/wiki/Star_of_Bethlehem "Star of Bethlehem"), or a white [dove](/wiki/Dove "Dove"), which can represent both the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") and [Peace](/wiki/Peace "Peace") on Earth. Other Christmas cards are more secular and can depict [Christmas traditions](/wiki/Christmas_tradition "Christmas tradition"), figures such as Santa Claus, objects directly associated with Christmas such as candles, holly, and baubles, or a variety of images associated with the season, such as Christmastide activities, snow scenes, and the wildlife of the northern winter.[[183]](#cite_note-184)

### Commemorative stamps

Main article: [Christmas stamp](/wiki/Christmas_stamp "Christmas stamp")

A number of nations have issued [commemorative stamps](/wiki/Commemorative_stamp "Commemorative stamp") at Christmastide.[[184]](#cite_note-185) Postal customers will often use these stamps to mail [Christmas cards](/wiki/Christmas_card "Christmas card"), and they are popular with [philatelists](/wiki/Philately "Philately").[[185]](#cite_note-186) These stamps are regular postage stamps, unlike [Christmas seals](/wiki/Christmas_seals "Christmas seals"), and are valid for postage year-round. They usually go on sale sometime between early October and early December and are printed in considerable quantities.

### Christmas seals

Main article: [Christmas seals](/wiki/Christmas_seals "Christmas seals")

Christmas seals were first issued to raise funding to fight and bring awareness to [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"). The first Christmas seal was issued in [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") in 1904, and since then other countries have issued their own Christmas seals.[[186]](#cite_note-187)

### Gift giving

Main article: [Christmas gift](/wiki/Christmas_gift "Christmas gift")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Gifts_xmas.jpg/330px-Gifts_xmas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gifts_xmas.jpg)

Christmas gifts under a Christmas tree

The exchanging of gifts is one of the core aspects of the modern Christmas celebration, making it the most profitable time of year for retailers and businesses throughout the world. On Christmas, people exchange gifts based on the Christian tradition associated with [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas"),[[187]](#cite_note-188) and the gifts of gold, frankincense, and myrrh which were given to the baby Jesus by the [Magi](/wiki/Biblical_Magi "Biblical Magi").[[188]](#cite_note-189)[[189]](#cite_note-190) The practice of gift giving in the [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") celebration of [Saturnalia](/wiki/Saturnalia "Saturnalia") may have influenced Christian customs, but on the other hand the Christian "core dogma of the [Incarnation](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)"), however, solidly established the giving and receiving of gifts as the structural principle of that recurrent yet unique event", because it was the Biblical Magi, "together with all their fellow men, who received the gift of God through man's renewed participation in the divine life".[[190]](#cite_note-Berking1999-191) However, Thomas J. Talley holds that the Roman Emperor [Aurelian](/wiki/Aurelian "Aurelian") placed the alternate festival on December 25 in order to compete with the growing rate of the Christian Church, which had already been celebrating Christmas on that date first.[[191]](#cite_note-Talley1991-192)

#### Gift-bearing figures

Main article: [List of Christmas and winter gift-bringers](/wiki/List_of_Christmas_and_winter_gift-bringers "List of Christmas and winter gift-bringers")

A number of figures are associated with Christmas and the seasonal giving of gifts. Among these are [Father Christmas](/wiki/Father_Christmas "Father Christmas"), also known as [Santa Claus](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus") (derived from the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") for Saint Nicholas), Père Noël, and the [Weihnachtsmann](/wiki/Weihnachtsmann "Weihnachtsmann"); [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas") or [Sinterklaas](/wiki/Sinterklaas "Sinterklaas"); the [Christkind](/wiki/Christkind "Christkind"); Kris Kringle; [Joulupukki](/wiki/Joulupukki "Joulupukki"); [tomte/nisse](/wiki/Nisse_(folklore) "Nisse (folklore)"); Babbo Natale; [Saint Basil](/wiki/Basil_of_Caesarea "Basil of Caesarea"); and [Ded Moroz](/wiki/Ded_Moroz "Ded Moroz"). The Scandinavian tomte (also called nisse) is sometimes depicted as a [gnome](/wiki/Gnome "Gnome") instead of Santa Claus.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Sinterklaas_2007.jpg/250px-Sinterklaas_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sinterklaas_2007.jpg)

[Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas"), known as [Sinterklaas](/wiki/Sinterklaas "Sinterklaas") in the Netherlands, is considered by many to be the original [Santa Claus](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus").[[192]](#cite_note-SewardLal2006-193)

The best known of these figures today is the red-dressed Santa Claus, of diverse origins. The name 'Santa Claus' can be traced back to the Dutch *Sinterklaas* ('Saint Nicholas'). Nicholas was a 4th-century [Greek](/wiki/Greeks_in_Turkey#History "Greeks in Turkey") bishop of [Myra](/wiki/Myra "Myra").[[193]](#cite_note-194)[[194]](#cite_note-Collins,_Ace_2009_121-195) Among other saintly attributes, he was noted for the care of children, generosity, and the giving of gifts. His feast day, December 6, came to be celebrated in many countries with the giving of gifts.[[45]](#cite_note-ADS-46)

Saint Nicholas traditionally appeared in bishop's attire, accompanied by helpers, inquiring about the behaviour of children during the past year before deciding whether they deserved a gift or not. By the 13th century, Saint Nicholas was well known in the Netherlands, and the practice of gift-giving in his name spread to other parts of central and southern Europe. At the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") in 16th- and 17th-century Europe, many Protestants changed the gift bringer to the Christ Child or *Christkindl*, corrupted in English to 'Kris Kringle', and the date of giving gifts changed from December 6 to Christmas Eve.[[45]](#cite_note-ADS-46)

The modern popular image of Santa Claus, however, was created in the United States, and in particular in New York. The transformation was accomplished with the aid of notable contributors including [Washington Irving](/wiki/Washington_Irving "Washington Irving") and the [German-American](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") cartoonist [Thomas Nast](/wiki/Thomas_Nast "Thomas Nast") (1840–1902). Following the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), some of the inhabitants of New York City sought out symbols of the city's non-English past. New York had originally been established as the Dutch colonial town of [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") and the Dutch Sinterklaas tradition was reinvented as Saint Nicholas.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

Current tradition in several [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") countries holds that while Santa makes the toys, he then gives them to the Baby Jesus, who is the one who actually delivers them to the children's homes, a reconciliation between traditional [religious beliefs](/wiki/Religion "Religion") and the [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography") of Santa Claus imported from the United States.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Thronendes_Jesuskind_BNM.jpg/250px-Thronendes_Jesuskind_BNM.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thronendes_Jesuskind_BNM.jpg)

[Christkind](/wiki/Christkind "Christkind"), Munich, Germany

In Italy's [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), Austria, the Czech Republic, Southern Germany, Hungary, Liechtenstein, Slovakia, and Switzerland, the [Christkind](/wiki/Christkind "Christkind") ([Ježíšek](/wiki/Je%C5%BE%C3%AD%C5%A1ek "Ježíšek") in Czech, Jézuska in Hungarian and Ježiško in Slovak) brings the presents. Greek children get their presents from [Saint Basil](/wiki/Saint_Basil "Saint Basil") on New Year's Eve, the eve of that saint's liturgical feast.[[196]](#cite_note-197) The German St. Nikolaus is not identical with the Weihnachtsmann (who is the German version of Santa Claus / Father Christmas). St. Nikolaus wears a bishop's dress and still brings small gifts (usually candies, nuts, and fruits) on December 6 and is accompanied by [Knecht Ruprecht](/wiki/Knecht_Ruprecht "Knecht Ruprecht"). Although many parents around the world routinely teach their children about Santa Claus and other gift bringers, some have come to reject this practice, considering it [deceptive](/wiki/Paternalistic_deception "Paternalistic deception").[[197]](#cite_note-198)

Multiple gift-giver figures exist in Poland, varying between regions and individual families. St Nicholas (*Święty Mikołaj*) dominates Central and North-East areas, the Starman (*Gwiazdor*) is most common in [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland"), Baby Jesus (*Dzieciątko*) is unique to [Upper Silesia](/wiki/Upper_Silesia "Upper Silesia"), with the Little Star (*Gwiazdka*) and the Little Angel (*Aniołek*) being common in the South and the South-East. Grandfather Frost (*Dziadek Mróz*) is less commonly accepted in some areas of Eastern Poland.[[198]](#cite_note-199)[[199]](#cite_note-200) It is worth noting that across all of Poland, St Nicholas is the gift giver on [Saint Nicholas Day](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas_Day "Saint Nicholas Day") on December 6.

### Sport

Christmas during the Middle Ages was a public festival with annual indulgences, including sporting.[[45]](#cite_note-ADS-46) When Puritans outlawed Christmas in England in December 1647 the crowd brought out footballs as a symbol of festive misrule.[[55]](#cite_note-auto-56) The Orkney [Christmas Day Ba'](/wiki/Christmas_Day_Ba%27 "Christmas Day Ba'") tradition continues.[[200]](#cite_note-201) In the [former top tier of English football](/wiki/Football_League "Football League"), home and away Christmas Day and Boxing Day double headers were often played guaranteeing football clubs large crowds by allowing many working people their only chance to watch a game.[[201]](#cite_note-bbcbs-202) Champions [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End "Preston North End") faced [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa "Aston Villa") [on Christmas Day 1889](/wiki/1889-90_Football_League "1889-90 Football League")[[202]](#cite_note-203) and the last December 25 fixture was in [1965 in England](/wiki/1965-66_in_English_football "1965-66 in English football"), [Blackpool](/wiki/1965-66_Blackpool_F.C. "1965-66 Blackpool F.C.") beating Blackburn Rovers 4–2.[[201]](#cite_note-bbcbs-202) One of the most memorable images of the [Christmas truce](/wiki/Christmas_truce "Christmas truce") during World War I was the games of football played between the opposing sides on Christmas Day 1914.[[203]](#cite_note-204)

## Choice of date

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/ChristAsSol.jpg/250px-ChristAsSol.jpg)](/wiki/File:ChristAsSol.jpg)

A mosaic dated to around 300 AD in the [Tomb of the Julii](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_Julii "Tomb of the Julii"), an apparently Christian tomb in the [Vatican Necropolis](/wiki/Vatican_Necropolis "Vatican Necropolis"). Most scholars believe it depicts Jesus as the sun god [Sol](/wiki/Sol_(Roman_mythology) "Sol (Roman mythology)") / [Helios](/wiki/Helios "Helios").[[204]](#cite_note-205)[[205]](#cite_note-206)

### Theories

Main article: [Date of the birth of Jesus § Day of birth](/wiki/Date_of_the_birth_of_Jesus#Day_of_birth "Date of the birth of Jesus")

There are several theories as to why December 25 was chosen as the date for Christmas. However, according to theology professor Susan Roll, liturgical historians agree that it was linked in some way with "the sun, the winter solstice and the popularity of solar worship in the later Roman Empire".[[206]](#cite_note-Roll107-207) In Roman tradition, December 25 was deemed to be the [winter solstice](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice") and March 25 the [spring equinox](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox"), dates that retained significance despite Julian calendar drift.[[191]](#cite_note-Talley1991-192)[[207]](#cite_note-:2-208) Greco-Roman writers in the second and third centuries called December 25 the birth day of the Sun.[[208]](#cite_note-209)[[209]](#cite_note-210) The early Church linked Jesus to the Sun and referred to him as the 'true Sun' or 'Sun of Righteousness'.[[210]](#cite_note-hijmans-211) In the early fifth century, [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") and [Maximus of Turin](/wiki/Maximus_of_Turin "Maximus of Turin") preached that it was fitting to celebrate Christ's birth at the winter solstice, because it marked the point when the hours of daylight begin to grow.[[211]](#cite_note-212)[[212]](#cite_note-213) The use of solar imagery for Christ is understood as part of broader cosmological symbolism in late antiquity, and may not have been direct cultic borrowing.[[191]](#cite_note-Talley1991-192)[[207]](#cite_note-:2-208)[[213]](#cite_note-:3-214)

The ['history of religions' or 'substitution' theory](/wiki/Date_of_the_birth_of_Jesus#History_of_religions_hypothesis "Date of the birth of Jesus") proposes that the Church chose December 25 as Christ's birthday (*dies Natalis Christi*)[[214]](#cite_note-215) to appropriate the Roman winter solstice festival *dies Natalis Solis Invicti*, the birthday of the god *[Sol Invictus](/wiki/Sol_Invictus "Sol Invictus")* (the 'Invincible Sun'). It had been held on this date since 274 AD; before the earliest evidence of Christmas on December 25.[[31]](#cite_note-Forsythe-32)[[32]](#cite_note-Bradshaw-33) Professor Gary Forsythe says that the *Natalis Solis Invicti* followed "the seven-day period of the *[Saturnalia](/wiki/Saturnalia "Saturnalia")* (December 17–23), Rome's most joyous holiday season since [Republican times](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), characterized by parties, banquets, and exchanges of gifts".[[31]](#cite_note-Forsythe-32) Roll says that "the specific nature of the relation" between Christmas and the *Natalis Solis Invicti* has not yet been "conclusively proven from extant texts".[[206]](#cite_note-Roll107-207)

The ['calculation theory'](/wiki/Date_of_the_birth_of_Jesus#Calculation_hypothesis "Date of the birth of Jesus") proposes that the date arose from Christian chronography rather than from an effort to supplant a pagan festival.[[32]](#cite_note-Bradshaw-33) It was first proposed by [Louis Duchesne](/wiki/Louis_Duchesne "Louis Duchesne"). Some early Christians marked [Jesus's death](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") on the day before [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover"), the [14th of Nisan](/wiki/Quartodecimanism "Quartodecimanism"). Several third‑century sources correlate this with the spring equinox on March 25. Duchesne conjectured that early Christians believed Jesus was conceived and died on the same date, yielding a March 25 conception and December 25 birth nine months later.[[191]](#cite_note-Talley1991-192)[[215]](#cite_note-Roll_87_89-216)[[216]](#cite_note-217) However, he admitted that this belief is not found in any early Christian text.[[215]](#cite_note-Roll_87_89-216) The earliest attestation of a December 25 Nativity may be contemporaneous with or earlier than clear attestations of a December 25 Sol Invictus festival.[[207]](#cite_note-:2-208)[[213]](#cite_note-:3-214)[[217]](#cite_note-218)

### Date according to Julian calendar

Some jurisdictions of the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), including those of [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox_Church "Georgian Orthodox Church"), [North Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonian_Orthodox_Church "Macedonian Orthodox Church"), [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegrin_Orthodox_Church "Montenegrin Orthodox Church"), [Serbia](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church"), and [Jerusalem](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Jerusalem "Eastern Orthodox Patriarchate of Jerusalem"), mark feasts using the older [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"). Since Christmas 1899 until Christmas 2099 inclusive, there is a difference of 13 days between the Julian calendar and the modern [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"). As a result, December 25 on the Julian calendar currently corresponds to January 7 on the calendar used by most governments and people in everyday life. Therefore, the aforementioned Orthodox Christians mark December 25 (and thus Christmas) on the day that is internationally considered to be January 7.[[218]](#cite_note-Jan7-219)

However, following the [Council of Constantinople in 1923](/wiki/Council_of_Constantinople_(1923) "Council of Constantinople (1923)"),[[219]](#cite_note-nationalgeographic-220) other Orthodox Christians, such as those belonging to the jurisdictions of [Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgarian_Orthodox_Church "Bulgarian Orthodox Church"), [Greece](/wiki/Church_of_Greece "Church of Greece"), [Romania](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church"), [Antioch](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church_of_Antioch "Greek Orthodox Church of Antioch"), [Alexandria](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Greek Orthodox Church of Alexandria"), [Albania](/wiki/Orthodox_Autocephalous_Church_of_Albania "Orthodox Autocephalous Church of Albania"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Church_of_Cyprus "Church of Cyprus"), [Finland](/wiki/Finnish_Orthodox_Church "Finnish Orthodox Church"), and the [Orthodox Church in America](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_in_America "Orthodox Church in America"), among others, began using the [Revised Julian calendar](/wiki/Revised_Julian_calendar "Revised Julian calendar"), which at present corresponds exactly to the Gregorian calendar.[[220]](#cite_note-4Dates-221) Therefore, these Orthodox Christians mark December 25 (and thus Christmas) on the same day that is internationally considered to be December 25.

The [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church") continues the original ancient [Eastern Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") practice of celebrating the birth of Christ not as a separate holiday, but on the same day as the celebration of his baptism ([Theophany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday)#Oriental_Orthodox "Epiphany (holiday)")), which is on January 6. This is a public holiday in Armenia, and it is held on the same day that is internationally considered to be January 6, because since 1923 the Armenian Church in Armenia has used the Gregorian calendar.[[221]](#cite_note-222) However, there is also a small [Armenian Patriarchate of Jerusalem](/wiki/Armenian_Patriarchate_of_Jerusalem "Armenian Patriarchate of Jerusalem"), which maintains the traditional Armenian custom of celebrating the birth of Christ on the same day as Theophany (January 6), but uses the Julian calendar for the determination of that date. As a result, this church celebrates "Christmas" (more properly called Theophany) on the day that is considered January 19 on the Gregorian calendar in use by the majority of the world.[[222]](#cite_note-223)

Following the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), Ukraine officially moved its Christmas date from January 7 to December 25, to distance itself from the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") that had supported Russia's invasion.[[223]](#cite_note-ukraine-date-224)[[224]](#cite_note-225) This followed the [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") formally adopting the [Revised Julian calendar](/wiki/Revised_Julian_calendar "Revised Julian calendar") for fixed feasts and solemnities.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

## Economy

Main article: [Economics of Christmas](/wiki/Economics_of_Christmas "Economics of Christmas")

Christmas is typically a peak selling season for retailers in many nations around the world; sales increase dramatically during this time as people purchase gifts, decorations, and supplies to celebrate. In the United States, the "Christmas shopping season" starts as early as October.[[230]](#cite_note-231) In Canada, merchants begin advertising campaigns before [Halloween](/wiki/Halloween "Halloween") (October 31) and step up their marketing following Remembrance Day on November 11. In the UK and Ireland, the Christmas shopping season starts from mid-November, around the time when high street [Christmas lights](/wiki/Christmas_lights "Christmas lights") are turned on.[[231]](#cite_note-232)[[232]](#cite_note-gar-233) A concept devised by retail entrepreneur [David Lewis](/wiki/David_Lewis_(English_merchant) "David Lewis (English merchant)"), the first [Christmas grotto](/wiki/Santa%27s_workshop "Santa's workshop") opened in [Lewis's](/wiki/Lewis%27s "Lewis's") department store in Liverpool, England in 1879.[[233]](#cite_note-234) In the United States, it has been calculated that a quarter of all personal spending takes place during the Christmas/holiday shopping season.[[234]](#cite_note-235) Figures from the [US Census Bureau](/wiki/US_Census_Bureau "US Census Bureau") reveal that expenditure in department stores nationwide rose from $20.8 billion in November 2004 to $31.9 billion in December 2004, an increase of 54 percent. In other sectors, the pre-Christmas increase in spending was even greater, there being a November–December buying surge of 100 percent in bookstores and 170 percent in jewelry stores. In the same year employment in American retail stores rose from 1.6 million to 1.8 million in the two months leading up to Christmas.[[235]](#cite_note-236) Industries completely dependent on Christmas include [Christmas cards](/wiki/Christmas_card "Christmas card"), of which 1.9 billion are sent in the United States each year, and live Christmas trees, of which 20.8 million were cut in the US in 2002.[[236]](#cite_note-237) In the UK in 2010, up to £8 billion was expected to be spent online at Christmas, approximately a quarter of total retail festive sales.[[232]](#cite_note-gar-233)

In most Western nations, Christmas Day is the least active day of the year for business and commerce; almost all retail, commercial and institutional businesses are closed, and almost all industries cease activity (more than any other day of the year), whether laws require such or not. In [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), the [Christmas Day (Trading) Act 2004](/wiki/Christmas_Day_(Trading)_Act_2004 "Christmas Day (Trading) Act 2004") prevents all large shops from trading on Christmas Day. Similar legislation was approved in Scotland in 2007. [Film studios](/wiki/Film_studio "Film studio") release many high-budget movies during the holiday season, including Christmas films, fantasy movies or high-tone dramas with high production values to hopes of maximizing the chance of nominations for the [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards").[[237]](#cite_note-238)

One economist's analysis calculates that despite increased overall spending, Christmas is a [deadweight loss](/wiki/Deadweight_loss "Deadweight loss") under orthodox [microeconomic theory](/wiki/Microeconomic_theory "Microeconomic theory"), because of the effect of gift-giving. This loss is calculated as the difference between what the gift giver spent on the item and what the gift receiver would have paid for the item. It is estimated that in 2001, Christmas resulted in a $4 billion deadweight loss in the US alone.[[238]](#cite_note-Deadweight-239)[[239]](#cite_note-econ-240) Because of complicating factors, this analysis is sometimes used to discuss possible flaws in current microeconomic theory. Other deadweight losses include the effects of Christmas on the environment and the fact that material gifts are often perceived as [white elephants](/wiki/White_elephant "White elephant"), imposing cost for upkeep and storage and contributing to clutter.[[240]](#cite_note-241)

## Prohibition

Main article: [Christmas controversies](/wiki/Christmas_controversies "Christmas controversies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Bezbozhnik_u_stanka_-_Run_along%2C_Lord%2C_1931%2C_n._22.jpg/250px-Bezbozhnik_u_stanka_-_Run_along%2C_Lord%2C_1931%2C_n._22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bezbozhnik_u_stanka_-_Run_along,_Lord,_1931,_n._22.jpg)

A 1931 edition of the Soviet magazine *[Bezbozhnik](/wiki/Bezbozhnik_(magazine) "Bezbozhnik (magazine)")*, published by the [League of Militant Atheists](/wiki/League_of_Militant_Atheists "League of Militant Atheists"), depicting an Orthodox Christian priest being forbidden to take home a tree for the celebration of [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide"), which was banned under the Marxist–Leninist doctrine of [state atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism")[[241]](#cite_note-Harper1999-242)

Christmas has been the subject of controversy and attacks from various sources, both Christian and non-Christian. Historically, it was prohibited by [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") during their ascendency in the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") (1647–1660) and in [Colonial New England](/wiki/Colonial_New_England "Colonial New England") where the Puritans outlawed the celebration of Christmas in 1659 on the grounds that Christmas was not mentioned in Scripture and therefore violated the [regulative principle of worship](/wiki/Regulative_principle_of_worship "Regulative principle of worship").[[242]](#cite_note-puritans-uk-243)[[243]](#cite_note-puritans-usa-244) The [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"), which was dominated by [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), passed a series of acts outlawing the observance of Christmas between 1637 and 1690; Christmas Day did not become [a public holiday in Scotland](/wiki/Christmas_in_Scotland "Christmas in Scotland") until 1871.[[60]](#cite_note-scotland-1871-61)[[244]](#cite_note-Daniels89-245)[[245]](#cite_note-246) Today, some conservative Reformed denominations such as the [Free Presbyterian Church of Scotland](/wiki/Free_Presbyterian_Church_of_Scotland "Free Presbyterian Church of Scotland") and the [Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_of_North_America "Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America") likewise reject the celebration of Christmas based on the regulative principle and what they see as its non-Scriptural origin.[[246]](#cite_note-247)[[247]](#cite_note-248) Celebrating Christmas is banned in the [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), as the [Governing Body](/wiki/Governing_Body_of_Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Governing Body of Jehovah's Witnesses") believes that Christmas is originally pagan and without basis in Scripture.[[248]](#cite_note-249) Christmas celebrations have also been prohibited by [atheist states](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism") such as the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")[[249]](#cite_note-250) and in 2015 by the majority Muslim states of [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan") and [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei").[[250]](#cite_note-christmas2015bans-251)

As Christmas celebrations began to spread globally even outside traditional [Christian cultures](/wiki/Christian_culture "Christian culture"), several Muslim-majority countries began to ban the observance of Christmas, claiming it undermined [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam").[[251]](#cite_note-252) In December 2018, the [Iraqi](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") Council of Ministers amended the national holidays law in order to designate Christmas Day (December 25) an official nationwide holiday which is to be celebrated by all Iraqis instead of only the country's [Christian minority](/wiki/Iraqi_Christians "Iraqi Christians").[[252]](#cite_note-253) In 2023, public Christmas celebrations were cancelled in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"), the city synonymous with the birth of Jesus. [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") leaders of various Christian denominations cited the [ongoing Israel–Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") in their unanimous decision to cancel celebrations.[[253]](#cite_note-254)

The government of the People's Republic of China officially espouses state atheism,[[254]](#cite_note-255) and has conducted [antireligious campaigns](/wiki/Antireligious_campaigns_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Antireligious campaigns of the Chinese Communist Party") to this end.[[255]](#cite_note-BuangChew2014-256) In December 2018, officials raided Christian churches prior to Christmastide and coerced them to close; Christmas trees and Santa Clauses were also forcibly removed.[[256]](#cite_note-BBC2018-257)[[257]](#cite_note-258)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Several branches of [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") that use the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") also celebrate on December 25 according to that calendar, which is now January 7 on the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"). Armenian Churches observed the nativity on January 6 even before the Gregorian calendar originated. Most Armenian Christians use the Gregorian calendar, still celebrating Christmas Day on January 6. Some Armenian churches use the Julian calendar, thus celebrating Christmas Day on January 19 on the Gregorian calendar, with January 18 being Christmas Eve. Some regions also celebrate primarily on December 24, rather than December 25.
